explanation: Answer: B | A centrifugal pump operating far from its BEP (off its curve) suffers increased radial thrust loading on the shaft and bearings, internal recirculation at low flows or recirculation/cavitation at high flows, elevated vibration, increased power consumption or cavitation damage, and accelerated seal and bearing wear. Prolonged off-BEP operation significantly reduces pump life.

question_text: Centrifugal pumps are classified as:
explanation: Answer: C | Centrifugal pumps are classified as dynamic (kinetic) machines because they add energy to the fluid continuously through centrifugal force and impeller rotation, rather than trapping and compressing a fixed volume of fluid. Flow rate varies with system pressure. Positive displacement pumps (gear, piston, diaphragm) trap and displace fixed volumes per stroke or revolution.

question_text: A positive displacement pump:
option_c: Delivers constant flow regardless of pressure
explanation: Answer: C | Positive displacement pumps deliver a nearly constant flow rate per revolution or stroke regardless of system pressure (within design limits). Examples include gear pumps, piston pumps, diaphragm pumps, and vane pumps. They must have a pressure relief valve in the discharge line because they will build pressure to the point of mechanical failure if the discharge is blocked.

question_text: Multistage pumps have:
explanation: Answer: A | Multistage centrifugal pumps have multiple impellers arranged in series on a common shaft, with the discharge of each stage feeding the inlet of the next. Each stage adds head (pressure), so the total head is the sum of all stages. Multistage pumps are used where high pressure is required (boiler feedwater, high-pressure water injection, pipeline pumps).

question_text: The main advantage of mechanical seals over packing is:
explanation: Answer: C | Mechanical seals provide virtually zero leakage (typically just a thin film for lubrication of the seal faces, often invisible as vapor). Compression packing always requires a controlled drip rate for lubrication. Mechanical seals also reduce shaft sleeve wear, lower power consumption, and are required for pumping hazardous or toxic fluids where leakage cannot be tolerated.

question_text: A balanced mechanical seal:
option_a: Reduces hydraulic closing force on faces
explanation: Answer: A | A balanced mechanical seal is designed with a step in the shaft or sleeve so that the hydraulic closing force acting on the seal face is reduced. The balance ratio (typically 0.7-0.8) limits the closing force to prevent excessive face wear and heat generation at high pressures. Balanced seals are used for pressures above approximately 100-150 psi.

question_text: The rotating seal face:
explanation: Answer: A | In a mechanical seal, the rotating seal face (also called the primary ring) is attached to and rotates with the shaft. It runs against the stationary seat (mating ring) which is fixed in the seal gland. The lapped, flat mating faces (typically carbon vs. ceramic, or tungsten carbide vs. silicon carbide) create the primary leak barrier. The extremely thin fluid film between faces provides lubrication.

question_text: O-rings in mechanical seals:
explanation: Answer: A | O-rings in mechanical seals provide static sealing at secondary seal points  between the rotating face and shaft (or sleeve), and between the stationary face and gland plate. They do not rotate (or rotate very slowly during axial movement) and prevent leakage along the shaft and between seal components. O-ring material is selected for chemical compatibility with the process fluid.

question_text: When installing a mechanical seal, cleanliness is:
explanation: Answer: C | Cleanliness is critical when installing mechanical seals because even microscopic particles on the polished seal faces can cause scratches that lead to leakage. Seal faces (typically carbon/ceramic or tungsten carbide/silicon carbide pairs) are lapped to within 3 helium light bands of flatness. Handle faces with lint-free gloves; clean with isopropyl alcohol.

question_text: Compression packing should be installed:
explanation: Answer: D | Compression packing rings must be installed with staggered joints (each ring rotated 90° or 120° from the previous one) to prevent a straight leak path through the joints. Each ring should be seated individually before adding the next. Never install all rings at once. Use the correct packing size and material for the application.

question_text: Proper packing leak-off rate is:
explanation: Answer: A | Compression packing requires a controlled drip rate (typically 40-60 drops per minute or more, per manufacturer guidelines) for lubrication and cooling of the shaft sleeve. A completely dry stuffing box causes excessive heat, scoring of the shaft sleeve, and rapid packing failure. Adjust gland follower gradually to achieve the correct leak rate without over-tightening.

question_text: The most common type of rolling element bearing is:
explanation: Answer: D | Deep groove ball bearings are the most common type of rolling element bearing due to their versatility, low friction, ability to carry both radial and moderate thrust loads, availability in a wide range of sizes, and relatively low cost. They are used in motors, pumps, gearboxes, and virtually all rotating machinery.

question_text: ABEC ratings refer to:
explanation: Answer: B | ABEC (Annular Bearing Engineers Committee) ratings define bearing precision/tolerance classes. ABEC 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9 are standard grades  higher numbers indicate tighter tolerances on bore, OD, width, and runout. ABEC 1 is standard industrial grade; ABEC 7 and 9 are used in precision machine tools and high-speed applications.

question_text: A tapered roller bearing:
option_d: Carries combined radial and thrust loads
explanation: Answer: D | Tapered roller bearings carry combined radial and axial (thrust) loads simultaneously. The tapered geometry of the rollers and raceways generates an axial component of force, so they must be mounted in opposing pairs (face-to-face or back-to-back) to handle thrust in both directions. They are adjustable by varying the preload between the opposing bearing pair.

question_text: Bearing preload:
explanation: Answer: A | Bearing preload removes internal clearance by applying a compressive load between the rolling elements and races. This eliminates play, increases stiffness, improves running accuracy, and reduces noise. Preload is used in pairs of angular contact or tapered roller bearings. Excessive preload causes overheating and early failure; insufficient preload allows vibration and reduced accuracy.

question_text: A cylindrical roller bearing:
explanation: Answer: A | Cylindrical roller bearings carry very heavy radial loads due to the line contact between the cylindrical rollers and the flat raceways  far greater contact area than ball bearings. Most types (NU, NJ, N) carry little or no axial (thrust) load. They are used in gearboxes, electric motors, and rolling mills where high radial capacity and rigidity are needed.

question_text: The inner race of a bearing is typically mounted:
explanation: Answer: D | The inner race (ring) is mounted with an interference (press) fit on the rotating shaft so it rotates with the shaft without creeping. The outer race is mounted with a clearance or slight interference fit in the housing, depending on whether the outer ring rotates or is stationary. Creep (spinning) of either ring causes rapid wear and heat generation.

question_text: Bearing failure due to contamination shows:
explanation: Answer: C | Contamination by hard particles (dirt, metal chips) causes false brinelling and indentations (denting) on the bearing races and rolling elements. These indentations create stress concentrations that initiate fatigue spalling. Contamination is the leading cause of premature bearing failure. Proper sealing, clean installation practices, and clean lubricants are essential.

question_text: A self-aligning bearing:
option_d: Compensates for shaft deflection and misalignment
explanation: Answer: D | Self-aligning ball bearings have a spherically curved outer raceway that allows the inner ring and ball assembly to tilt up to 2-3 degrees relative to the outer ring. This compensates for shaft deflection under load, housing bore misalignment, and mounting errors. They are used on long shafts prone to deflection, conveyor pulleys, and agricultural equipment.

question_text: When pressing a bearing onto a shaft, force should be applied to:
explanation: Answer: C | When pressing a bearing onto a shaft, force must be applied only to the inner race  never through the rolling elements or cage. Applying force to the outer race would transmit it through the rolling elements, indenting (brinelling) the races and causing immediate bearing damage. Use a proper bearing press sleeve that contacts only the inner race.

question_text: Bearing C3 clearance indicates:
explanation: Answer: D | Bearing internal clearance class C3 indicates greater than normal (CN) internal clearance between the rolling elements and races. C3 is specified when the bearing will experience tight interference fits, elevated operating temperatures, or both  conditions that reduce internal clearance. C2 = less than normal; CN = normal; C3 and C4 = greater than normal.

question_text: The basic dynamic load rating (C) of a bearing:
option_b: Is the load for 1 million revolutions with 90% reliability
explanation: Answer: B | The basic dynamic load rating (C) is the constant load under which a group of identical bearings will achieve a basic rating life (L10) of 1 million revolutions with 90% reliability (10% failure rate). It is used in bearing life calculations: L10 = (C/P)^p x 10^6 revolutions, where P = equivalent dynamic load and p = 3 for ball bearings, 10/3 for roller bearings.

question_text: Proper bearing storage requires:
explanation: Answer: C | Bearings must be stored in their original sealed packaging to prevent contamination and corrosion. Store horizontally (flat) to prevent the grease from migrating to one side of sealed bearings. Maintain controlled temperature (15-25°C) and relative humidity below 60%. Avoid areas with vibration, which can cause false brinelling in stored bearings. Rotate old stock first (FIFO).

question_text: The induction heating method for bearing installation:
explanation: Answer: D | Induction heating uses an electromagnetic field to heat the bearing inner race rapidly and uniformly, causing it to expand thermally so it slides onto the shaft without hammering. Heat to 80-100°C maximum (never exceed 120°C, which can alter the steel's hardness). Use a demagnetizer after heating. This method prevents brinelling damage from impact installation.

question_text: Bearing operating temperature should be monitored because:
explanation: Answer: D | Excessive bearing temperature (above 80-90°C for most grease-lubricated bearings) indicates problems such as over-lubrication, under-lubrication, contamination, overloading, misalignment, or insufficient clearance. Monitor with infrared thermometer or thermocouple. Rule of thumb: if bearing is too hot to hold (>60°C), investigate the cause.

question_text: A pillow block bearing:
option_c: Is a housed bearing unit for shaft support
explanation: Answer: C | A pillow block (plummer block) is a self-contained, housed bearing unit consisting of a bearing inserted into a cast iron or steel housing with a flat base for mounting. It supports a shaft externally and is bolted to a frame or structure. The housing protects the bearing and typically includes a lubrication fitting. Pillow blocks simplify installation and allow shaft height adjustment by shimming.

question_text: Bearing seals protect against:
explanation: Answer: B | Bearing seals (contact seals) and shields (non-contact) serve a dual purpose: they prevent contaminants (dirt, water, metal particles) from entering the bearing, and they retain the lubricant (grease or oil) inside the bearing. Contact seals provide better exclusion but generate more friction and heat. Contamination and lubricant loss are the two leading causes of premature bearing failure.

question_text: Viscosity is:
option_b: A measure of fluid's resistance to flow
explanation: Answer: B | Viscosity is a fluid's resistance to flow (internal friction). Higher viscosity oils are thicker and flow more slowly; lower viscosity oils are thinner. ISO VG (Viscosity Grade) numbers refer to kinematic viscosity in centistokes (cSt) at 40°C. Selecting the correct viscosity for operating temperature and speed is critical for adequate lubrication film thickness.

question_text: NLGI grade refers to:
explanation: Answer: D | NLGI (National Lubricating Grease Institute) grades classify grease consistency/stiffness from 000 (very soft, semi-fluid) to 6 (very hard, block grease). NLGI 2 is the most common industrial grade  firm enough to stay in place but soft enough to flow to bearing contact areas. The thickener type (lithium, calcium, polyurea) also affects performance.

question_text: In Canada, oxygen cylinders used for oxy-fuel cutting and welding are typically:
explanation: Answer: D | In Canada, oxygen cylinders are painted black per CSA/CGA standards. This differs from some other countries. Acetylene cylinders are typically red in Canada. Color coding is a secondary identification method only  always read the cylinder label and valve connection type as the primary identification. Never rely on color alone.

question_text: Acetylene cylinder pressure should not exceed:
explanation: Answer: A | Acetylene working pressure must never exceed 15 psi (103 kPa). Above this pressure, acetylene becomes unstable and can decompose explosively, independent of the oxygen supply. Acetylene cylinders contain the gas dissolved in acetone on a porous filler material  never lay acetylene cylinders on their side as this allows liquid acetone to flow into the regulator.

question_text: A flashback arrestor:
option_c: Prevents flame from traveling back into hoses
explanation: Answer: C | Flashback arrestors are safety devices installed on both the torch inlets and/or regulator outlets that contain a sintered metal filter and check valve. If a flashback (flame traveling back into the hose) occurs due to a blocked tip or reverse flow, the arrestor's thermal cutoff and check valve extinguish the flame and prevent it from reaching the cylinders.

question_text: When lighting an oxy-fuel torch, you should:
option_d: Open acetylene first, ignite, then add oxygen
explanation: Answer: D | Proper oxy-fuel torch lighting procedure: (1) Open acetylene valve slightly (1/4 to 1/2 turn), (2) ignite with a friction lighter (never matches or open flame), (3) adjust acetylene until black smoke disappears, (4) gradually open oxygen valve to achieve the correct flame type (neutral, carburizing, or oxidizing) for the application. Never use a lighter that requires thumb pressure near an open flame.

question_text: SMAW stands for:
explanation: Answer: A | SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc Welding), commonly called "stick welding," uses a consumable flux-coated electrode. The flux coating burns to create a shielding gas that protects the weld pool from atmospheric contamination, and forms slag that covers and protects the solidifying weld. SMAW is versatile, portable, and effective outdoors but slower than wire-feed processes.

question_text: Welding electrode E7018 has a tensile strength of:
explanation: Answer: C | Electrode classification E7018: E = electrode, 70 = 70,000 psi minimum tensile strength, 1 = all-position welding, 8 = low-hydrogen iron powder coating, DCEP or AC current. The "18" indicates a low-hydrogen electrode requiring storage in a rod oven at 250-300°F (120-150°C) to prevent moisture absorption and hydrogen-induced cracking.

question_text: Arc blow is caused by:
explanation: Answer: D | Arc blow is caused by magnetic fields in the workpiece or circuit that deflect the arc from its intended path. It occurs mainly with DC welding on ferromagnetic materials near the end of joints, at changes in section, or when using high amperage. Remedies include switching to AC, changing work lead position, welding toward the ground connection, or using a back-step technique.

question_text: Duty cycle on a welder indicates:
option_a: The percentage of 10 minutes it can operate at rated output
option_b: How long it can weld continuously
explanation: Answer: A | Duty cycle is the percentage of a 10-minute period that a welder can operate at its rated output current without overheating. Example: a 200A welder with 60% duty cycle can weld for 6 minutes out of every 10 minutes at 200A. Exceeding the duty cycle activates the thermal protection circuit. Duty cycle increases at lower amperage settings.

question_text: Annealing steel:
explanation: Answer: A | Annealing involves heating steel to above its critical temperature (austenizing), then very slowly cooling it (typically in the furnace) to produce a soft, ductile structure with relieved internal stresses. Annealing improves machinability, restores ductility after cold working, and relieves residual stresses from welding or forming operations.

question_text: Quenching steel means:
explanation: Answer: B | Quenching is the rapid cooling of steel from above its critical temperature (typically 800-900°C for medium carbon steel) in water, oil, or polymer quench media. The rapid cooling traps carbon in a distorted crystal structure called martensite, which is very hard and brittle. Quenching is followed by tempering to reduce brittleness while retaining hardness.

question_text: Carbon content in steel affects:
explanation: Answer: B | Carbon content is the most important factor affecting steel's hardness and strength. Low carbon steel (< 0.30% C) is soft, ductile, and weldable but cannot be hardened significantly by heat treatment. High carbon steel (0.60-1.0% C) can be hardened to high hardness but becomes more brittle and difficult to weld. Medium carbon steel (0.30-0.60% C) balances strength and toughness.

question_text: A spark test identifies:
option_b: Type of metal by spark pattern
explanation: Answer: B | A spark test identifies the approximate type and carbon content of steel by grinding a sample on a bench grinder and observing the spark pattern. High-carbon steel produces bright, bushy sparks with many star bursts (explosions). Wrought iron produces long, straight orange lines. Cast iron produces short, dull red sparks. Stainless steel produces long, wavy orange sparks. It is a field identification method, not a precise test.

question_text: The arrow side of a welding symbol is:
option_d: The side the arrow points to
explanation: Answer: D | The arrow side of a welding symbol indicates the joint side that the arrow physically points to. Weld information placed below the reference line applies to the arrow side; information above the line applies to the other side (far side). When the arrow points to the side where the weld is to be made, the weld symbol is placed below the reference line.

question_text: A flag symbol on a weld indicates:
explanation: Answer: A | A flag symbol (a filled triangular flag at the junction of the reference line and arrow) on a welding symbol indicates a field weld  a weld to be made at the installation site rather than in the fabrication shop. This distinguishes site welds from shop welds on assembly drawings, which is important for quality control, inspection scheduling, and cost tracking.

question_text: Non-destructive testing (NDT) includes:
explanation: Answer: A | Non-destructive testing (NDT) methods include: radiography (X-ray/gamma ray), ultrasonic testing (UT), magnetic particle testing (MT), liquid penetrant testing (PT/dye penetrant), eddy current testing, and visual inspection. These methods detect internal and surface flaws without damaging the part. Destructive tests (tensile, impact, hardness) require test specimens that are consumed in the test.

question_text: A quality management system ensures:
option_d: Consistent processes and standards are met
explanation: Answer: D | A quality management system (QMS) such as ISO 9001 establishes documented processes and standards to ensure products and services consistently meet customer and regulatory requirements. It includes procedures for process control, inspection, nonconformance management, corrective action, and continuous improvement. QMS reduces errors, improves consistency, and provides traceability.

question_text: A group of general rules adopted by a legal jurisdiction and made into law are called what?
explanation: Answer: B | Codes are legally binding rules adopted by a legal jurisdiction and enforced by law. Recommended Practices are optional industry guidelines. Standards are developed by professional bodies but may or may not be legally adopted. Only Codes carry the force of law within their jurisdiction.

question_text: Documents prepared by a professional group indicating good engineering practices that are optional rather than legally required are called what?
explanation: Answer: D | Recommended Practices are documents prepared by professional or industry groups that outline best engineering practices. They are advisory in nature and not legally binding unless adopted by a jurisdiction as code. This distinguishes them from Codes, which are legally enforceable.

question_text: The transfer of energy in the form of waves or particles through space or a medium is referred to as what?
explanation: Answer: C | Radiation is heat energy transferred through electromagnetic waves and does not require direct contact or a medium. Unlike conduction (requires direct contact) and convection (requires fluid movement), radiant heat can travel through a vacuum — as demonstrated by solar energy reaching Earth.

question_text: Baseboard heaters warm a room using which heat transfer method?
option_d: Convection — heating air that rises and circulates
explanation: Answer: D | Baseboard heaters warm rooms primarily through natural convection. The heating element heats air in contact with it; the warmer (less dense) air rises and cooler air falls to replace it, creating a circulation current that distributes heat throughout the room.

question_text: A Heat Transfer Unit (HTU) that has both a heating element and a surrounding cabinet is called what?
explanation: Answer: C | A Convector consists of a heating element (finned tube or electric element) enclosed inside a cabinet that directs airflow over the element to improve heat distribution. The cabinet design enhances the natural convection effect compared to a bare element or radiator.

question_text: How is baseboard radiation rated?
option_a: BTU per hour per square foot
option_c: BTU per hour per foot of length
explanation: Answer: C | Baseboard radiation is rated in BTU per hour per foot (BTU/hr/ft) of length. This rating allows engineers to select the correct length of baseboard to meet the calculated heat loss of a room at the design water temperature.

question_text: Unit heaters should be mounted how high above the floor?
explanation: Answer: A | Unit heaters should be mounted 6 to 12 feet above the floor. This height ensures the discharged air covers the intended floor area without creating excessive drafts at occupant level, and keeps the unit out of the working area while remaining serviceable.

question_text: Why are some air-handling units mounted on springs?
option_a: To isolate vibration from the building structure
explanation: Answer: A | Air-handling units are mounted on springs (vibration isolators) to prevent vibration from fans, motors, and rotating components from transmitting through the building structure. Without isolation, this vibration causes noise complaints and can fatigue structural connections over time.

question_text: What type of glycol is considered food grade and non-toxic for use in systems that may contact food or people?
explanation: Answer: B | Propylene Glycol is the food-grade non-toxic glycol approved for use in systems where incidental contact with food or humans is possible — such as snow melt, food processing facilities, and potable water-adjacent systems. Ethylene glycol is toxic and not approved for these applications.

question_text: What is the primary purpose of a heating system?
option_a: To maintain a comfortable temperature for occupants
explanation: Answer: A | The primary purpose of a heating system is individual comfort — maintaining a comfortable indoor temperature for the building occupants. All other functions (hot water generation, process heating) are secondary to this fundamental purpose.

question_text: What is the maximum length of a serpentine heating circuit using 3/4-inch diameter tubing?
explanation: Answer: C | The maximum length of a serpentine heating circuit using 3/4-inch tube is 500 feet. Exceeding this length increases pressure drop and reduces heat delivery at the far end of the circuit, leading to uneven heating and poor system performance.

question_text: What is the heat loss limitation of a series loop heating system?
explanation: Answer: D | The heat loss limitation of a series loop heating system is 60 to 70 MBH (thousand BTU per hour). In a series loop all terminal units are in series, so water cools progressively — limiting total capacity. Larger heat losses require multi-zone or reverse-return designs.

question_text: What are the two types of hot-water unit heaters?
explanation: Answer: C | The two types of hot-water unit heaters are Blow-Through (propeller fan pushes air through the coil) and Draw-Through (fan draws air through the coil). Draw-through is more efficient because the fan is in cooler air, but blow-through is simpler in construction.

question_text: Large heating coils located inside air handling units used to heat or cool an occupied space are called what?
explanation: Answer: B | Blast Coils are large coil assemblies installed inside air handling units (AHUs). Air is forced (blasted) across the coil surface by fans, providing high-capacity heating or cooling to the air stream that is then distributed throughout the building.

question_text: A snow melt system requires what specific component to function?
explanation: Answer: D | A snow melt system requires a Heat Exchanger (HEX) to transfer heat from the boiler water to the glycol solution circulating through the slab tubing. The glycol solution must be kept separate from the boiler water to prevent glycol contamination of the heating system.

question_text: On a vertical unit heater where is the fan located?
option_a: At the bottom below the coil
option_b: At the top above the motor
option_c: On the side of the cabinet
option_d: In the middle between the motor above and the heating coil below
explanation: Answer: D | On a vertical unit heater the fan is located in the middle — the motor is positioned above the fan and the heating coil is below. This arrangement allows the fan to draw air up through the coil and discharge it upward (or downward through deflectors) for proper air distribution.

question_text: The reversal of normal water flow caused by negative pressure in a system is called what?
explanation: Answer: A | Back-Siphonage occurs when negative (sub-atmospheric) pressure in the supply line causes water to flow backward from the receiving vessel into the supply line. This is a cross-connection hazard that can introduce contaminants into the potable water supply.

question_text: What is the most effective method of preventing cross-contamination between a potable water supply and a non-potable system?
explanation: Answer: C | An Air-Gap — a physical open vertical separation between the water supply outlet and the overflow level of the receiving vessel — is the most effective method of preventing cross-contamination. It is the only backflow prevention method that cannot fail to a non-protective position, since there is literally no physical connection.

question_text: If a 3-inch pipe is reduced to a 1.5-inch pipe how will that affect the velocity of the fluid flowing through the line?
explanation: Answer: A | When a 3-inch pipe is reduced to 1.5-inch, the cross-sectional area decreases by a factor of 4 (area varies as diameter squared: 3²/1.5² = 9/2.25 = 4). By continuity of flow (Q = A × V), velocity must increase 4 times to maintain the same flow rate.

question_text: How does increased pressure of a fluid affect its boiling point?
explanation: Answer: A | Increasing pressure increases the boiling point of a fluid. Greater pressure requires the fluid molecules to acquire more energy before they can escape into the vapor phase. This principle is used in pressurized hot water systems to maintain liquid water at temperatures above 100°C.

question_text: What causes water hardness?
option_d: Dissolved minerals — specifically calcium and magnesium salts
explanation: Answer: D | Water hardness is caused by dissolved minerals, specifically calcium and magnesium salts, that water picks up as it flows through rock and soil formations. Hard water causes scale buildup in boilers, pipes, and heat exchangers, reducing efficiency and eventually causing damage.

question_text: What causes temporary hardness of water and how is it removed?
option_a: Bicarbonates of sodium and magnesium — removed by boiling
explanation: Answer: A | Temporary hardness is caused by bicarbonates of calcium and magnesium. When water is boiled, the bicarbonates decompose and precipitate as calcium carbonate (the familiar white scale), removing the hardness. Permanent hardness (caused by sulfates and chlorides) cannot be removed by boiling alone.

question_text: The gases dissolved in rainwater make its pH above or below 7?
explanation: Answer: A | Rainwater dissolves carbon dioxide from the atmosphere forming carbonic acid (H2CO3), which makes its pH below 7 — slightly acidic (typically pH 5.6). This natural acidity makes rainwater corrosive to piping and boiler components if used untreated.

question_text: What section of the ASME code covers unfired pressure vessels?
explanation: Answer: C | ASME Section VIII covers unfired pressure vessels. Section I covers power boilers, Section IV covers heating boilers, and B31.3 covers process piping. Unfired pressure vessels include tanks, heat exchangers, and similar equipment that contain pressure but have no internal combustion.

question_text: How do free-standing steam radiators transfer heat into a room?
option_d: By both convective currents and radiation
explanation: Answer: D | Free-standing steam radiators transfer heat by both radiation and convection. The hot radiator surface radiates infrared energy directly to surrounding objects, while also heating the adjacent air which rises by natural convection and circulates throughout the room.

question_text: What is the term for cold air that enters a house through cracks and gaps in the building envelope?
explanation: Answer: D | Infiltration Heat Loss refers to uncontrolled cold air leakage into a building through cracks, gaps, and openings in the envelope (walls, windows, doors, foundation). It is a significant component of the total heating load and is reduced by air-sealing measures.

question_text: A device that holds back steam but allows condensate and gases to pass through is called what?
explanation: Answer: A | A Steam Trap is an automatic device that discriminates between steam and condensate/non-condensable gases. It allows condensate and gases to drain while retaining live steam in the distribution system. Without steam traps, condensate accumulates and can cause water hammer, reduced heat transfer, and system damage.

question_text: Too much condensate accumulating in a steam line can cause what serious problem?
explanation: Answer: C | Excessive condensate in a steam line causes Water Hammer — a violent shockwave when high-velocity steam traveling at hundreds of feet per minute meets a slug of liquid water. The collision produces a loud bang and extreme pressure spike that can rupture pipes, destroy fittings, and damage equipment.

question_text: What type of steam trap is actuated by differences in temperature between the condensate and the steam?
explanation: Answer: A | A Thermostatic steam trap operates based on the temperature difference between steam and condensate. The sensing element (bellows or bimetallic strip) responds to temperature — it opens to discharge cooled condensate and closes against hotter steam, maintaining efficient drainage.

question_text: Which type of steam trap is recommended for use on a low pressure steam main?
option_d: F and T (Float and Thermostatic) trap
explanation: Answer: D | The Float and Thermostatic (F&T) steam trap is recommended for low pressure steam mains. The float mechanism discharges condensate continuously as it accumulates, while the thermostatic air vent discharges non-condensable gases — making it ideal for main drip stations.

question_text: Sections of a cast-iron boiler are drawn together and held using what?
explanation: Answer: A | Sections of a cast-iron sectional boiler are drawn together and held in compression using Tie Rods — long steel rods that pass through all sections and are tightened with nuts to clamp the assembly together. The tight clamping compresses the sealing nipples between sections.

question_text: What allows water to travel between the sections of a cast-iron sectional boiler?
explanation: Answer: C | Water travels between sections of a cast-iron sectional boiler through Push Nipples and Sealing Rings. The push nipples fit into mating ports in adjacent sections and the sealing rings form a watertight seal around each nipple when the tie rods are tightened.

question_text: An aquastat senses changes in what property to control the heating system?
explanation: Answer: A | An aquastat is a temperature-sensing control device that monitors boiler or system water temperature. When water temperature falls below the setpoint it signals the burner to fire; when it reaches the high limit it shuts the burner off. It may also control circulator pumps and zone valves.

question_text: What prevents natural circulation from occurring in a hydronic heating system when circulation is not desired?
explanation: Answer: A | Flow Control Valves (gravity stop valves) prevent natural (thermosiphon) circulation in a hydronic system when the circulator pump is not running. They require the pump's pressure to open, ensuring water only flows when intentionally circulated — preventing unwanted heat migration to upper floors when the system is off.

question_text: A float-operated device that automatically vents air from a hydronic heating system is called what?
explanation: Answer: D | An Automatic Air Vent uses a float mechanism: when air is present, the float drops and opens the vent port, releasing the air. When water fills the chamber the float rises and closes the vent. These devices are installed at high points in the system where air naturally collects.

question_text: What does a micro bubble reabsorber do in a hydronic heating system?
option_b: Eliminates microscopic air bubbles circulating in the system water
explanation: Answer: B | A micro bubble reabsorber captures and removes the microscopic air bubbles that circulate throughout the hydronic system. These tiny bubbles are too small to be caught by conventional air separators. Eliminating them prevents noise, corrosion, and loss of heating efficiency.

question_text: What is a Lockshield valve used for in a hydronic heating system?
option_a: To act as the main shutoff
option_c: To balance flow in the system
explanation: Answer: B | A Lockshield valve has a removable protective cap covering the adjustment stem so that only authorized personnel with the appropriate tool can change the flow setting. Once balanced, the cap is replaced to prevent accidental or unauthorized adjustment that would disturb the system balance.

question_text: How are high temperature hydronic boilers rated?
option_d: MBH/BHP (thousand BTU per hour / Boiler Horsepower)
explanation: Answer: D | High temperature hydronic boilers are rated in MBH (thousands of BTU per hour) and/or BHP (Boiler Horsepower, where 1 BHP = 33,475 BTU/hr). These ratings define the maximum heat output the boiler can deliver to the system at design conditions.

question_text: What is the purpose of a Wild Loop in a hydronic heating system?
option_a: To prevent pump deadheading by providing a bypass path
option_c: To add extra heat to the system
explanation: Answer: A | A Wild Loop prevents pump deadheading by providing a continuously open bypass path through which the circulator can always move water, even when all zone valves are closed. Without it, a closed system would allow pressure to build excessively, potentially damaging the pump seal.

question_text: The expansion and contraction of a piping system due to temperature changes is controlled with what?
explanation: Answer: D | Expansion Loops (U-bends, Z-bends) and Expansion Joints (bellows, slip-type) are installed in piping systems to absorb the dimensional changes caused by thermal expansion and contraction. Without these devices, thermal stress would accumulate and could rupture pipe joints or damage equipment.

question_text: How would one describe a 3-way mixing valve in terms of its inlet and outlet configuration?
explanation: Answer: D | A 3-way mixing valve has 2 inlets and 1 outlet. Two water streams at different temperatures (typically hot supply and cold/return water) enter through the two inlets and are blended in proportions controlled by an actuator to produce one outlet stream at a desired mixed temperature.

question_text: A unit heater is often used in a hydronic heating system as what?
option_b: A Wild Loop providing both heat and pump protection
explanation: Answer: B | A unit heater in a hydronic system commonly serves the dual function of a Wild Loop — it provides useful space heating while simultaneously offering the circulator pump a path through which water can always flow, protecting against deadheading when zone valves close.

question_text: What should be done to prevent radiant heating tubes from collapsing when being covered with concrete?
option_a: Pressurize them to 30 PSI during the pour
explanation: Answer: A | To prevent collapse during a concrete pour, radiant heating tubes should be pressurized to 30 PSI and kept pressurized throughout the pour and curing process. The internal pressure keeps the flexible tube rigid, maintains its shape, and also proves the tubes are leak-free before being permanently buried.

question_text: What is the ideal floor surface temperature for a radiant floor heating system?
explanation: Answer: C | The ideal radiant floor surface temperature is 29°C / 85°F. This temperature is warm enough to efficiently heat the space by radiation to the occupants and room surfaces, but cool enough to remain comfortable underfoot for extended standing and walking.

question_text: What is one advantage of the reverse return heating system compared to other piping arrangements?
option_d: It requires no balancing valves because all circuits have approximately equal pipe length
explanation: Answer: D | The reverse return system is inherently easy to balance because every terminal unit circuit has approximately the same total pipe length (supply + return). This equal resistance means flow distributes naturally and evenly, reducing or eliminating the need for complex flow balancing.

question_text: What must be installed on a hydronic heating system because of the different flow velocities present?
explanation: Answer: B | Balancing Cocks (balancing valves) must be installed to equalize flow throughout the system. Different circuit lengths and fittings create different resistances, so without balancing, closer zones receive too much flow and far zones too little. Balancing valves are throttled to create equal resistance in all circuits.

question_text: What is the name of the special tees used in a one-pipe heating system to divert flow into and out of terminal units?
explanation: Answer: C | Monoflow Tees are specially designed tees used in one-pipe heating systems. Their internal geometry creates a pressure differential that diverts a portion of the main flow into each terminal unit supply, while a second monoflow tee at the outlet returns the cooled water back to the main.

question_text: What type of pumps are used in hydronic heating systems?
explanation: Answer: C | Centrifugal pumps are the standard choice for hydronic heating systems. They impart velocity to the water using a rotating impeller, converting kinetic energy to pressure. They are reliable, quiet, self-priming in wet conditions, and their flow-pressure curve is well-suited to variable hydronic system resistance.

question_text: A gauge that simultaneously indicates temperature pressure and feet of head is called what?
explanation: Answer: B | A Combination Gauge displays water temperature, system pressure, and equivalent feet of head all in one instrument. These gauges are commonly installed on hydronic system components — particularly near the pump and boiler — to give a complete picture of system status at a glance.

question_text: Hot water EDR (Equivalent Direct Radiation) is equal to how many BTU per hour per square foot?
explanation: Answer: C | One square foot of hot water EDR (Equivalent Direct Radiation) equals 150 BTU per hour. EDR is a standardized unit that allows different types of terminal units to be compared on a common basis. Steam EDR = 240 BTU/hr/sq ft; hot water EDR = 150 BTU/hr/sq ft.

question_text: On a hydronic system what might be the cause of a weeping (leaking) relief valve?
explanation: Answer: C | A waterlogged compression tank is the most common cause of a weeping relief valve. When the tank loses its air cushion and fills with water, it can no longer absorb thermal expansion — system pressure rises with each heating cycle until it reaches the relief valve setpoint, causing it to weep.

question_text: The balancing valves on a hydronic system are what type?
explanation: Answer: B | Globe valves are used as balancing valves on hydronic systems. Their design — with a disc that moves perpendicular to a seat in a throttling chamber — allows precise, repeatable flow adjustment over a wide range. Gate valves (full-open/full-closed) are not suitable for throttling.

question_text: What type of copper tubing is approved for use in hydronic heating applications?
option_c: Types K L and M are all approved depending on pressure and temperature requirements
explanation: Answer: C | Types K, L, and M copper tubing are all approved for hydronic heating applications. Type K has the thickest wall (highest pressure rating), Type L is medium wall (most common for heating), and Type M is the lightest wall (suitable for lower pressures). The selection depends on the specific system requirements.

question_text: What part of a hot water circulator transfers energy to the water?
explanation: Answer: C | The Impeller is the rotating component inside the circulator that transfers energy to the water. As it spins, it imparts centrifugal velocity to the water, converting rotational kinetic energy into both velocity and pressure — moving water through the system.

question_text: The PRV (Pressure Reducing Valve) in a residential hydronic system is set at what pressure?
explanation: Answer: A | The Pressure Reducing Valve (PRV) in a residential hydronic system is set at 15 PSI. This is the standard fill pressure that provides adequate static head to the highest point of the system while keeping the system well below the relief valve setting (typically 30 PSI).

question_text: What is the most popular type of plastic pipe used in hydronic heating systems?
explanation: Answer: A | PEX (cross-linked polyethylene) is the most popular plastic pipe for hydronic heating. Its cross-linked molecular structure gives it exceptional flexibility, temperature and pressure resistance, and the ability to handle repeated heating/cooling cycles without fatigue — making it ideal for radiant floor tubing and distribution piping.

question_text: With reference to hydronic heating what does the acronym ODT represent?
explanation: Answer: D | ODT stands for Outdoor Design Temperature — the coldest outdoor temperature used as the design basis for calculating the heating load of a building in a specific geographic location. All equipment is sized to maintain indoor comfort at the ODT.

question_text: What is the control system voltage on a heating boiler?
explanation: Answer: D | Boiler control systems operate at 24 Volts. Low voltage (24V) is the industry standard for safety controls, thermostats, and zone valves in heating systems. It reduces shock hazard, allows longer wire runs without significant voltage drop, and is compatible with the full range of thermostats and controls.

question_text: Where is coagulant added to boiler feedwater to produce floc?
explanation: Answer: A | Coagulant is added In the Clarifier tank where slow mixing allows it to contact all suspended particles. The coagulant (typically aluminum sulfate) destabilizes the colloidal particles causing them to aggregate into visible floc that then settles out or is removed by filtration.

question_text: What are the most common coagulants used in water treatment processes?
explanation: Answer: B | Aluminum Sulfate (alum) and Aluminum Hydroxide are the most commonly used coagulants in water treatment. When added to turbid water they form aluminum hydroxide floc that adsorbs and entraps colloidal particles, bacteria, and suspended matter, allowing them to settle or be filtered out.

question_text: What is used to regenerate sodium zeolite water softeners after their ion exchange capacity is exhausted?
explanation: Answer: C | Brine (concentrated salt / sodium chloride solution) is used to regenerate sodium zeolite softeners. The high sodium concentration in the brine reverses the ion exchange reaction, displacing the calcium and magnesium ions from the zeolite beads and replacing them with sodium ions, restoring softening capacity.

question_text: A sodium ion exchange water softener contains what as its active medium?
explanation: Answer: A | A sodium ion exchange water softener contains Zeolite Beads (ion exchange resin) as the active medium. As hard water passes through, the beads exchange calcium and magnesium ions (which cause hardness and scale) for sodium ions, producing softened water with no hardness minerals.

question_text: What causes a flow control valve to open?
option_d: Fluid Velocity — pressure differential created by the running circulator pump
explanation: Answer: D | A flow control valve opens when the running circulator pump creates flow and the resulting fluid velocity generates sufficient pressure differential across the valve to overcome its spring. When the pump stops and velocity drops, the spring closes the valve, preventing natural convection circulation.

question_text: What is the greatest advantage of hydronic heating systems over other types of heating?
option_d: Multiple Zoning — independent temperature control in different areas
explanation: Answer: D | The greatest advantage of hydronic heating is Multiple Zoning. The system can easily be divided into independently controlled zones using zone valves or separate circulators, providing different temperatures in different areas of a building from a single boiler — impossible with simpler heating systems.

question_text: What mechanism limits the amount of deflection or sagging in a suspended piping system?
explanation: Answer: C | A Pipe Guide limits lateral deflection in a suspended piping system by constraining the pipe so it can only move in the axial direction (along its length). This prevents bowing, sagging, and lateral vibration while still allowing the controlled thermal expansion for which the guide is designed.

question_text: A bare pipe resting on a support can be protected from wear and damage by installing what on the pipe?
explanation: Answer: B | A Wear Pad is a protective pad installed on the pipe at the point of contact with the support. It protects the pipe from abrasion caused by thermal movement across the support and prevents crevice corrosion at the metal-to-metal contact point.

question_text: What device prevents insulation on a pipe from being crushed when the pipe is suspended from a hanger?
explanation: Answer: C | An Insulation Saddle (pipe shoe or insulation shield) is a rigid curved insert installed inside the insulation at hanger locations. It transfers the pipe load to the hanger without compressing or cutting through the insulation, maintaining the insulation's thermal and vapor barrier integrity.

question_text: What is one advantage of the Gun Pakt expansion joint?
option_a: It can be repacked under pressure without shutting down the system
option_d: It has a longer service life than bellows
explanation: Answer: A | One key advantage of the Gun Pakt (packing gland) expansion joint is that it can be repacked under pressure — the packing gland can be tightened or the packing replaced while the system remains in service, minimizing costly shutdowns for maintenance.

question_text: Rainwater must be treated to remove what impurities before use in a boiler system?
explanation: Answer: A | Rainwater must be treated to remove dissolved Oxygen and Carbon Dioxide. These gases make rainwater acidic (carbonic acid from CO2) and promote aggressive corrosion. Dissolved oxygen is particularly damaging, causing pitting corrosion on boiler tubes, drums, and piping — it must be removed by deaeration.

question_text: What is another name for microfiltration in water treatment?
explanation: Answer: D | Another name for microfiltration in water treatment is Reverse Osmosis (RO). In RO, pressure forces water through a semi-permeable membrane, removing dissolved minerals, salts, bacteria, and other contaminants at the molecular level — producing very high-purity water for boiler and process applications.

question_text: When installed properly a wire rope clip develops what percentage of the wire rope's full strength?
explanation: Answer: C | A properly installed wire rope clip (U-bolt type) develops 80% of the wire rope's rated breaking strength. Achieving this requires: correct number of clips per rope diameter, proper spacing, correct torque, and the U-bolt saddle placed on the dead (tail) end. Improperly applied clips dramatically reduce this efficiency.

question_text: The bringing together of various lifting devices to raise or lower a workpiece is called what?
explanation: Answer: D | Rigging is the coordinated process of planning and assembling all the equipment (slings, shackles, blocks, hooks, spreader bars) needed to safely attach, lift, move, and position a load. A qualified rigger selects the appropriate components, calculates loads, and directs the lift.

question_text: Can anyone erect a tube and clamp scaffold on a construction site?
option_a: Yes any worker may do it
option_b: No — only Qualified Personnel are permitted
explanation: Answer: B | Only Qualified Personnel may erect tube and clamp scaffold. This type of scaffold requires specialized knowledge of load calculations, bracing configurations, and component compatibility. Improper assembly creates a serious fall and collapse hazard. Regulations require that scaffold erection be performed or directly supervised by a qualified person.

question_text: What is the maximum length of a scaffold ladder?
explanation: Answer: B | The maximum length of a scaffold ladder is 6.4 metres (21 feet). Longer ladders must be broken into separate sections with landing platforms between them. This limit ensures the ladder can be safely used and prevents excessive flex and deflection in longer single-section ladders.

question_text: What is used on scaffolds to prevent materials tools or debris from falling on workers below?
explanation: Answer: D | Toe Boards are installed along the open edges of scaffold platforms to prevent tools, materials, and debris from being accidentally kicked off or rolling off the edge onto workers below. They typically stand 4 inches (100 mm) minimum above the platform surface.

question_text: A worker must wear a harness when working over how many metres above the floor or ground?
explanation: Answer: A | Workers are required to wear a full-body fall protection harness connected to a fall arrest system when working more than 3 metres (approximately 10 feet) above the floor, ground, or nearest lower level. This is a regulatory requirement under provincial occupational health and safety legislation.

question_text: A rope is attached to a post or ring using what type of connection?
explanation: Answer: C | A Hitch is a temporary connection used to attach a rope to a post, ring, pipe, or other object. Unlike a knot tied in the rope itself, a hitch wraps around the object and can be easily untied when no longer under load. Common hitches include the clove hitch, half hitch, and timber hitch.

question_text: What is the most commonly used synthetic rope in industry?
explanation: Answer: B | Polypropylene rope is the most commonly used synthetic rope in industrial applications. It is lightweight, floats on water (useful for marine and wet environments), resists most chemicals, and is relatively inexpensive. However, it is weaker than nylon and degrades with prolonged UV exposure.

question_text: What is the strongest type of synthetic rope available?
explanation: Answer: B | Nylon is the strongest common synthetic rope. It has high tensile strength, excellent shock absorption due to its elasticity (10-15% stretch under load), and good resistance to abrasion and most chemicals. Its elasticity makes it valuable where shock loads are possible, though the stretch can be problematic in lifting applications.

question_text: Why is hemp rope not used in industrial applications?
option_d: It may rot under wet conditions weakening it unpredictably
explanation: Answer: D | Hemp rope is not used industrially because it is susceptible to rot when exposed to moisture. Natural fiber ropes absorb water, promoting mold and bacterial decay that progressively weakens the fibers — often invisibly from the outside. This unpredictable strength loss makes hemp unsuitable for safety-critical lifting applications.

question_text: The centre or core of a wire rope is called what?
explanation: Answer: B | The Core is the central foundation element of a wire rope around which the outer strands are wound. The core provides support to the outer strands under load, maintains the rope's round cross-section, and contributes to the rope's resistance to crushing and deformation during use.

question_text: What can be used as a brake to help control the position of a load floating on an air cushion?
explanation: Answer: C | A Tirfor (hand-operated cable winch / come-along) can be used as a controlled brake on a load floating on an air cushion. The air film virtually eliminates friction, so even a slight incline can cause uncontrolled movement. The Tirfor provides the controlled resistance needed to position the load precisely.

question_text: What principle states that a body immersed in a fluid loses as much weight as the weight of the fluid it displaces?
explanation: Answer: C | Archimedes' Principle states that a body immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces. This explains why large heavy objects can float and why objects appear lighter when submerged — the principle is fundamental to marine rigging and submerged pipe installation.

question_text: What is the buoyant force acting on a 5 cubic foot box that is fully immersed in water?
explanation: Answer: C | The buoyant force on a 5 cubic foot box immersed in water is 312 lbs. Calculation: volume × weight of water per cubic foot = 5 ft³ × 62.4 lbs/ft³ = 312 lbs. This is the upward force the water exerts on the submerged object regardless of the object's own weight or material.

question_text: What is the preferred way of attaching a tag line to a load during a lift?
option_b: With a shackle through a hole
explanation: Answer: C | A Clove Hitch is the preferred method for attaching a tag line to a load. It is quick to tie, grips the load securely under tension, can be tied around any shape, and importantly can be untied quickly after the lift. Tag lines allow riggers to guide the load without being directly under it.

question_text: What is the term for the helical twists put into the individual strands of wire rope during manufacture?
explanation: Answer: A | Preforming is the process of permanently shaping wire rope strands into their helical (spiral) form before they are wound into the finished rope. Preformed rope is more flexible, less prone to unraveling when cut, and the wires lie in a stress-free natural position — significantly improving handling and fatigue life.

question_text: What is the name of a hand-operated cable winch used with wire rope for pulling and tensioning?
explanation: Answer: A | A Tirfor is a hand-operated ratchet-grip wire rope winch. It grips the wire rope between two cam jaws that alternately grip and advance the rope with each stroke of the handle — capable of pulling loads of several tons. It is used for both pulling and holding loads and can also be operated in reverse.

question_text: A knot tied in a rope decreases the rope's working strength by what percent?
explanation: Answer: C | A knot reduces rope strength by approximately 50%. The sharp bends and compression forces at the knot concentrate stress, causing the rope to fail at roughly half its rated breaking strength. This is why splices (which reduce strength by only 5-10%) are preferred to knots in critical rigging applications.

question_text: What can be used to change the direction of wire rope during a lift?
explanation: Answer: C | A Snatch Block is used to redirect wire rope during a lift. It is a pulley block that opens on one side, allowing the wire rope to be inserted without threading the end through. By changing rope direction, it can also provide mechanical advantage when reeved in a multi-part system.

question_text: What is the formula for calculating the safe working load of a sling (where D is wire rope diameter in inches)?
explanation: Answer: D | The safe working load (SWL) formula for a wire rope sling is: SWL (tons) = D² × 6, where D is the wire rope diameter in inches. Example: a 1-inch rope has SWL = 1² × 6 = 6 tons. A 1/2-inch rope has SWL = 0.25 × 6 = 1.5 tons. This formula applies to a single-leg vertical hitch.

question_text: Which is the strongest natural fiber rope?
explanation: Answer: B | Hemp is the strongest natural fiber rope, followed by manila, sisal, and cotton. Despite being the strongest natural fiber, hemp has been almost entirely replaced by synthetic ropes in industrial use due to its susceptibility to rot when wet and the availability of far stronger synthetic alternatives.

question_text: What is the rule of thumb for calculating the approximate weight of steel pipe?
option_a: A 1-foot length of 1-inch pipe weighs 1 lb per inch of diameter
option_b: A 1-inch cube of steel weighs approximately 40 lbs
option_c: Weight equals OD squared divided by 4
option_d: A 1-inch x 1-inch x 1-foot bar weighs 3.4 lbs
explanation: Answer: B | The rule of thumb is: a 1-inch × 1-inch × 1-inch piece of steel weighs approximately 40 lbs per cubic foot (or 0.283 lb/in³). This ratio is used to estimate the weight of steel components from their volume. The actual density of steel is 490 lbs/ft³ or 0.2833 lb/in³.

question_text: The ability of a material to withstand two equal forces acting in opposite directions is referred to as what?
explanation: Answer: C | Shear Strength is the ability of a material to resist forces that act parallel to a cross-section, tending to slide one part past another. In rigging, shear failure can occur at pin holes, bolt connections, and where slings bear against sharp edges. Shear strength is typically lower than tensile strength for most materials.

question_text: Shoulder type eyebolts should be used with angular pulls greater than what degree from vertical?
explanation: Answer: C | Shoulder type eyebolts must be used whenever the angle of pull exceeds 45 degrees from vertical. Standard plain (non-shouldered) eyebolts are rated for vertical (0 degree) pulls only. Angular loading on a plain eyebolt bends the eye and can reduce its capacity by 75% or more — leading to catastrophic failure.

question_text: How can you tell if a chain is suitable and safe for use in construction lifting?
option_c: By visual inspection for rust only
option_d: By the stamp or marking on each link identifying its grade
explanation: Answer: D | Each link of a certified lifting chain is Stamped with identifying marks (letters and/or numbers) indicating the grade (alloy), manufacturer, and load rating. Grade 80 and Grade 100 alloy steel chains are approved for overhead lifting. Unstamped chains must never be used for lifting — they have no known load rating.

question_text: What is the minimum number of rollers that should be used when moving a heavy skid?
explanation: Answer: B | A minimum of three rollers must be used when moving a heavy skid on rollers. Two rollers support the load while the third is placed ahead for the skid to roll onto. As the rear roller comes free it is moved to the front — maintaining at least two rollers under the load at all times for stability.

question_text: A hydraulic jack with a separate pump that allows for different ram sizes to be used is called what?
explanation: Answer: A | A Porta Power is a portable hydraulic jack system consisting of a separate hand-operated hydraulic pump connected by a hose to interchangeable ram attachments of different capacities and configurations. This versatility makes it ideal for confined spaces and specialized lifting, spreading, or pressing tasks.

question_text: How many Imperial gallons are contained in one cubic foot of volume?
explanation: Answer: D | One cubic foot of volume contains 6.24 Imperial gallons. This conversion factor is used in calculations involving tank volumes, pipe capacities, and flow rates. Note that 1 cubic foot contains 7.48 US gallons (not Imperial) — so be careful to specify the type of gallon being used.

question_text: What is the ability of a material to resist forces acting to pull it apart referred to as?
explanation: Answer: C | Tensile Strength is the maximum stress a material can withstand when being pulled or stretched before it breaks. It is expressed in PSI or MPa and is a fundamental property used to select wire rope, chain, shackles, and structural components for rigging. Wire rope is rated by its minimum breaking force based on tensile strength.

question_text: How much force is required to properly torque a lock collar?
option_a: Full wrench torque per manufacturer spec
explanation: Answer: B | A lock collar must be tightened to Fingertight only — turned by hand until resistance is felt, without tools. Using a wrench overtightens the collar, which can crush or damage the shaft, distort the collar bore, or prevent it from seating correctly. The design relies on hand torque for the correct clamping force.

question_text: What type of copper tubing is specifically approved for heat tracing applications?
option_d: Types K and L are approved for heat tracing
explanation: Answer: D | Type K and Type L copper tubing are approved for heat tracing applications. Their wall thickness provides adequate durability for the small-diameter tubing used in steam and hot water tracing circuits, where sustained temperature and pressure cycling requires a reliable material.

question_text: How could one improve heat transfer between steam tracing and the process line being traced?
option_a: Apply Heat-Conducting Paste between the tracing and the process pipe
explanation: Answer: A | Applying Heat-Conducting Paste (also called thermal compound) between the steam tracing tube and the process pipe fills the air gaps at the contact points. Air is an excellent insulator, so even a thin air gap dramatically reduces heat transfer. The paste has a much higher thermal conductivity than air and significantly improves the overall heat transfer rate.

question_text: What is the easiest way to trace a small bore vertical pipe?
option_a: Spiral the tracing around the pipe
option_c: Run a single straight trace along the pipe
option_d: Clamp the tracer to one side only
explanation: Answer: A | Spiraling the tracing line around the pipe (helical wrap) is the easiest method for small bore vertical pipes. The spiral wrap ensures heat is applied evenly around the full circumference and along the entire length of the pipe, preventing cold spots and ensuring the process fluid or pipe content stays at the required temperature.

question_text: Can PVC pipe be fusion welded?
option_a: No — PVC cannot be welded at all
option_d: Yes — PVC can be fusion welded using heat to melt and fuse the joint
explanation: Answer: D | Yes, PVC pipe can be fusion welded using heat. Unlike polyethylene which requires butt fusion or electrofusion, PVC can also be joined by solvent cement. However, fusion welding of PVC is used in specific applications where solvent cementing is not appropriate, using heating tools to melt and fuse the pipe ends together.

question_text: Can PE (polyethylene) pipe be solvent welded?
option_b: No — PE cannot be solvent welded and must be joined by fusion welding or mechanical fittings
explanation: Answer: B | Polyethylene (PE) pipe cannot be solvent welded. Its chemical resistance to solvents — one of its key advantages — makes it immune to the dissolving action of solvent cements. PE must be joined by butt fusion welding, electrofusion fittings, or approved mechanical compression or flange fittings.

question_text: What is the simplest way of joining fiberglass pipe and fittings?
explanation: Answer: B | The simplest method of joining FRP (fiberglass reinforced plastic) pipe is Hand Layup or the Contact Mould Method. Alternating strips of fiberglass mat saturated with catalyzed resin are applied over the joint by hand, building up layers of structural laminate. The method requires minimal equipment and no heat.

question_text: What is the name for the strands formed by spinning a quantity of glass fibers together?
explanation: Answer: C | Rovings are continuous bundles of glass filaments formed by gathering and lightly twisting many individual glass fiber strands together. They are used as the primary structural reinforcement in filament-wound fiberglass pipe. Rovings can be used as-is or chopped for spray-up applications.

question_text: What is the main contaminant found in surface water that must be treated before use?
explanation: Answer: C | The main contaminant in surface water is Suspended Solids — particles of silt, clay, organic matter, algae, and debris carried in the water. These must be removed by coagulation, settling, and filtration before the water can be used in process systems, boilers, or potable water applications.

question_text: Why is specialty purpose piping needed in industrial applications?
option_d: For corrosion prevention where standard carbon steel cannot handle corrosive chemicals
explanation: Answer: D | Special purpose piping is required for Corrosion Prevention. In chemical plants, pulp mills, and other industries, many process fluids are highly corrosive to standard carbon steel. Specialty materials such as stainless steel, lined pipe, FRP, plastic, and exotic alloys provide the chemical compatibility and corrosion resistance needed.

question_text: Why does the oil and gas industry prefer plastic piping over metal in many applications?
option_c: Resistance to wax buildup — crude oil deposits wax inside metal pipes while plastic has better resistance
explanation: Answer: C | The oil and gas industry uses plastic piping largely because crude oil and natural gas can deposit wax (paraffin) on the interior of metal pipes, restricting flow. Plastic pipe has better resistance to wax adhesion, reducing plugging frequency and maintenance costs in production and gathering systems.

question_text: Elastomer pipe liners are made from what natural material?
option_d: Rubber — natural or synthetic rubber bonded to the pipe interior
explanation: Answer: D | Elastomer pipe liners are made from Rubber — natural or synthetic elastomers bonded or compressed into the interior of a metal pipe. The rubber liner provides excellent chemical resistance, erosion resistance, and resilience against particle impact, protecting the pipe wall from corrosive or abrasive process fluids.

question_text: What material is the least expensive type of pipe liner?
explanation: Answer: C | Polypropylene is the least expensive pipe liner material. It offers good chemical resistance to acids, bases, and most solvents at moderate temperatures, making it the cost-effective choice for many chemical service applications where a more expensive liner material is not required.

question_text: Can lined pipe be welded?
option_a: Yes — lined pipe can be welded provided the liner integrity is maintained
option_c: No — welding always destroys the liner
option_d: Only by cutting out and replacing the liner section
explanation: Answer: A | Lined pipe can be welded, provided care is taken not to damage the liner during the process. Typically the liner is pulled back from the weld zone, the pipe is welded, and then the liner is reinstalled or new liner material is applied at the joint. Some liners (like PTFE) use special insert fittings that are not affected by welding.

question_text: What is another name for catalysts used in the fiberglassing process?
explanation: Answer: A | Catalysts used in fiberglass work are also called Hardeners. They are added to the polyester or epoxy resin to initiate and accelerate the chemical cross-linking (curing) reaction that converts the liquid resin to a solid. The most common catalyst for polyester resin is MEKP (methyl ethyl ketone peroxide).

question_text: What is the most exotic and expensive piping material in use today?
explanation: Answer: D | Titanium is the most exotic and expensive piping material in common industrial use. Its exceptional corrosion resistance (comparable to platinum in many environments), high strength-to-weight ratio, and biocompatibility make it invaluable in highly aggressive chemical service, offshore oil production, and specialized aerospace applications despite its very high cost.

question_text: The process of forming a protective oxide layer on the surface of stainless steel is called what?
explanation: Answer: C | Passivating is the chemical treatment process that promotes and strengthens the natural chromium oxide passive layer on stainless steel surfaces. After fabrication or welding, the passive layer may be disturbed or contaminated. Passivation (typically using nitric or citric acid) cleans the surface and re-establishes the protective layer.

question_text: Should stainless steel piping be stress-relieved after fabrication?
option_b: Yes — but only for large bore pipe
option_c: No — stress relief can sensitize stainless steel and destroy its corrosion resistance
explanation: Answer: C | Stainless steel should NOT be stress-relieved. Heating stainless steel in the temperature range of 425–850°C (793–1562°F) causes sensitization — carbon migrates to grain boundaries and combines with chromium, depleting the chromium from the grain boundary zone and making the steel susceptible to intergranular corrosion.

question_text: What type of valve is mounted between flanges and held in place entirely by the flange bolts?
explanation: Answer: C | A Wafer Type valve is designed to sit between two pipe flanges with no integral flanges of its own. It is held in position and sealed entirely by the flange bolts that clamp through the flanges on each side of the valve. This design is much more compact and lightweight than a traditional flanged valve.

question_text: What post-weld treatment is required on nickel alloys to maintain their corrosion resistance after welding?
option_c: Passivating — restoring the protective oxide layer
explanation: Answer: C | Passivating is required after welding nickel alloys. The heat of welding disturbs the protective oxide layer on nickel alloy surfaces, leaving them susceptible to corrosion. Chemical passivation (using appropriate acid solutions) restores the passive film and returns the metal to its design corrosion resistance.

question_text: What is the primary element that gives stainless steel its corrosion resistance?
explanation: Answer: A | Chromium is the primary alloying element responsible for stainless steel's corrosion resistance. When present at 10.5% or more by weight, chromium reacts with oxygen to form a thin, stable, self-repairing chromium oxide passive layer on the surface that prevents further oxidation and corrosion.

question_text: Stainless steel is an alloy made of what metals?
explanation: Answer: B | Stainless steel is an alloy of Nickel, Chromium, and Iron as its primary constituents. The chromium (minimum 10.5%) provides corrosion resistance through the passive oxide layer, nickel improves toughness, ductility, and high-temperature performance, and iron is the base metal. Other elements (molybdenum, manganese) are added in specific grades.

question_text: A substance composed of two or more metals combined together is called what?
explanation: Answer: B | An Alloy is a metallic material composed of two or more elements, at least one of which is a metal. Alloys are created to produce properties superior to those of the individual elements — such as greater strength, corrosion resistance, or specific electrical properties. Stainless steel, brass, and bronze are common alloys.

question_text: What exotic metal is known for both exceptional chemical resistance and flammability characteristics?
explanation: Answer: A | Titanium is known for both exceptional chemical resistance (resistant to most acids and seawater) and for its flammability under certain conditions. Fine titanium powder, shavings, and turnings can ignite and burn with extreme intensity. This dual characteristic requires careful handling and cutting practices in fabrication.

question_text: Stainless pipe is made from what metals?
explanation: Answer: B | Stainless steel pipe is made from Chromium, Nickel, and Iron. The chromium content (typically 18%) provides corrosion resistance via the passive oxide layer, nickel (typically 8-10% in austenitic grades) adds toughness, formability, and high-temperature strength, and iron forms the structural base.

question_text: How is the strongest type of fiberglass pipe constructed?
option_d: Filament Winding — continuous fibers wound at precise angles under tension around a mandrel
explanation: Answer: D | Filament Wound fiberglass pipe is the strongest type. Continuous glass fiber rovings are wound under controlled tension at precise angles (typically ±55° for pressure pipe) around a rotating mandrel with resin applied simultaneously. This creates a highly oriented fiber structure with maximum strength-to-weight ratio.

question_text: When working with fiberglass and using MEKP catalyst what should never be added directly to it?
option_c: A Promoter (accelerator) — mixing promoter directly with MEKP creates an explosive reaction
explanation: Answer: C | A Promoter (cobalt naphthenate) must NEVER be added directly to MEKP catalyst. The combination of these two chemicals in concentrated form creates a violently exothermic reaction that can cause fire or explosion. The correct procedure is to add promoter to the resin first, mix thoroughly, then add the MEKP separately.

question_text: What safety equipment must always be worn when working with MEKP?
explanation: Answer: B | Monogoggles (chemical splash goggles providing a complete seal around the eyes) must always be worn when working with MEKP. MEKP is a strong oxidizer and organic peroxide that causes severe and potentially permanent eye damage on contact. Standard safety glasses do not provide adequate splash protection.

question_text: When using the hand lay-up method of joining FRP pipe which strip must be applied first?
option_b: The Narrow Strip — placed at the joint centerline to create the initial bond
explanation: Answer: B | In the hand lay-up method, the Narrow Strip is applied first at the joint centerline. This initial narrow strip bridges the two pipe ends and creates the first structural bond across the joint gap. Successively wider strips are then applied over it to build up the required laminate thickness and structural strength.

question_text: If one doubles the amount of catalyst added to FRP resin how does that affect the gel time?
option_b: Reduces the gel time by one half
option_c: Has no effect on gel time
explanation: Answer: B | Doubling the catalyst (MEKP) concentration reduces the gel time by approximately one half. The catalyst initiates the free-radical polymerization reaction; more catalyst means more initiation sites, accelerating the reaction and causing the resin to gel in half the normal time. Temperature also greatly affects gel time — higher temperatures speed curing.

question_text: What hazards might a fitter encounter when working on a demineralizer?
option_b: Caustic and Acid — the regeneration chemicals are highly corrosive
explanation: Answer: B | When working on a demineralizer (ion exchange water treatment unit), a fitter may encounter Caustic (sodium hydroxide, NaOH) and Acid (typically hydrochloric or sulfuric acid) used to regenerate the cation and anion exchange resins respectively. These chemicals are highly corrosive and require full PPE including face shield, chemical-resistant gloves, and protective clothing.

question_text: Why is silicon added to cast iron during its production?
explanation: Answer: B | Silicon is added to cast iron primarily to improve its Corrosion Resistance. High-silicon cast iron (Duriron, 14.5% Si) is highly resistant to sulfuric acid and many other corrosive chemicals. Silicon also promotes the formation of graphite flakes and improves castability during production.

question_text: Name one advantage of using glass pipe in a piping system.
option_a: Corrosion Resistance — glass is chemically inert and resistant to virtually all acids bases and solvents
explanation: Answer: A | The primary advantage of glass pipe is its outstanding Corrosion Resistance. Glass is chemically inert and resistant to virtually all acids (except hydrofluoric acid), bases, solvents, and oxidizing agents. This makes it ideal for laboratory drain lines, pharmaceutical processes, and chemical plants handling aggressive fluids.

question_text: How often must a glass piping system be supported along its length?
option_d: At every change of direction only
explanation: Answer: B | A glass piping system must be supported At Every Joint. Glass is brittle and has low tensile and bending strength — it cannot span the distances that metal pipe can without excessive deflection and stress. Supporting at every joint provides the close spacing needed to prevent breakage from the pipe's own weight.

question_text: Kimax is what type of pipe?
option_c: Glass pipe — borosilicate glass pipe with thermal and chemical resistance
